chosen_title: How the fake-GST-invoice (fake ITC) racket works
caption:
A supplier you've never met could be pocketing GST — and leaving you to face the raid.

Fake ITC scams work like this: shell companies register on the GST portal — on paper only, no warehouse, no staff, no real goods. They issue invoices to real businesses, collect GST from them, and never deposit a single rupee with the government. The buyer claims Input Tax Credit on tax the government never received.

You could be three links down the chain and completely innocent — but the department traces the network back to you. 📌 Illustration: ₹50L purchase, ₹9L GST (18%) claimed as ITC. Supplier turns out to be a shell firm. Outcome: ₹9L reversed + ₹2.16L interest (24% p.a. for one year) + up to ₹9L penalty = ₹20L+ exposure. (Numbers are illustrative only.) For ITC fraud above ₹5 crore, Section 132 of the CGST Act allows arrest.

The fix is simple: verify every supplier's GSTIN on the portal, cross-check your GSTR-2B before filing, and never claim ITC that isn't already reflecting in your statement.

What to do: search your top suppliers on gstin.gov.in right now — their filing status is visible in seconds.

Got an ITC mismatch or GST notice? WhatsApp us — we'll tell you exactly where you stand.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: CGST Act, Sections 16, 74, 122 and 132 (publicly available legislation).

slides.headline: The GST scam that's getting innocent businesses raided

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.headline: How the fake-invoice chain is built
slides.body: Shell companies register on the GST portal with valid GSTINs — but no warehouse, no workers, no actual goods. They issue invoices, collect GST from real buyers, and vanish without depositing a rupee with the government. The buyer claims Input Tax Credit on tax that was never paid.

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.headline: You might be the last link — and not even know it
slides.body: Your supplier looks legitimate: GSTIN, invoice, everything ticks. But two links up the chain sits a shell firm. The department traces the network back to you and reverses your ITC. 📌 Illustration: ₹50L purchase, ₹9L GST claimed as ITC. Supplier found to be a shell. Exposure: ₹9L reversed + ₹2.16L interest (24% p.a.) + up to ₹9L penalty = ₹20L+ hit. Illustrative only — actual outcomes vary.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: The penalty on fraudulent ITC under Section 122 of the CGST Act equals the full tax amount — on top of 24% annual interest. For fraud above ₹5 crore, Section 132 allows arrest. What started as a supplier shortcut becomes a criminal case for the business at the end of the chain.

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.title: 5 ways to keep your ITC clean
points: Look up every new supplier's GSTIN on the GST portal and check whether they are actively filing returns — it takes 30 seconds.
Only claim ITC that is already reflecting in your GSTR-2B; never claim on the basis of a paper invoice alone.
Watch for red flags: brand-new registrations, round-number invoices, no physical address, and no traceable business presence.
Keep e-way bill records for every inward supply — proof that goods actually moved is your strongest defence.
If you receive an ITC mismatch or scrutiny notice, respond before the deadline with full invoice, payment, and receipt documentation.
